meta.title: Poetry Collection — Vocabulary Set 5
meta.subject: English Vocabulary
meta.description: Challenge set — antonyms, word families, metaphor, advanced usage across all 16 words.

questions.question: What is the OPPOSITE of 'deep'?
questions.explanation: The opposite of 'deep' (extending far down) is 'shallow' (not very deep). A shallow pond is only ankle deep; a deep ocean extends far, far down.

questions.question: What is the OPPOSITE of 'zoom' (move very fast)?
questions.explanation: The opposite of 'zoom' (move very fast) is 'crawl very slowly.' Interestingly, 'crawl' is another word from the poetry vocabulary — and it represents the slowest movement!

questions.question: Match each word to its OPPOSITE.
questions.explanation: Antonyms: deep ↔ shallow, great ↔ small, squishy ↔ hard/firm, zoom ↔ crawl/move slowly. Knowing opposites deepens understanding of each word.

questions.question: 'Decorate,' 'decoration,' and 'decorative' all come from the same root word.
questions.explanation: Yes! 'Decorate' (verb) → 'decoration' (noun) → 'decorative' (adjective). All three come from the Latin root 'decor' meaning beauty or ornament.

questions.question: Sort these words — BASE WORDS or CHANGED FORMS?
questions.explanation: 'Carve,' 'decorate,' and 'scoop' are base verbs. 'Carved' and 'scooped' are past-tense forms. 'Decoration' is a noun form (thing) created from the verb 'decorate.'

questions.question: The poem calls the box a 'cardboard CAVE.' This is a metaphor. Why is 'cave' used instead of 'box'?
options: Because caves and boxes are the same thing
Because 'cave' creates a more exciting, imaginative image — both are dark and hollow inside
Because the box was found in a cave
questions.explanation: Both caves and boxes are hollow and dark inside. By calling it a 'cardboard cave,' the poem makes the box feel more adventurous and exciting. Metaphors connect two things through shared qualities.

questions.question: A 'dragon' exists in real life — you could find one in a shadowy forest.
questions.explanation: Dragons are mythical — they exist only in stories, poems, and imagination. You cannot find a real dragon in a forest! In 'The Box,' the dragon is a product of the child's creative imagination.

questions.question: Which sentence uses BOTH 'might' and 'deep' correctly?
options: It might deep in the ocean.
It might be very deep — we cannot see the bottom of this lake.
questions.explanation: 'It might be very deep' — 'might' expresses possibility, 'deep' describes the lake's depth. 'We cannot see the bottom' confirms why depth is uncertain. Both words used correctly.

questions.question: Arrange the words to create a sentence about imagination:
questions.explanation: Correct sentence: 'It might be very fun to build your own castle.' Using 'might' to express a possibility — building a castle (like in Sandcastle) MIGHT be a wonderful idea!

questions.question: Sort these vocabulary words — are they things that EXIST IN REAL LIFE or only in IMAGINATION/STORIES?
questions.explanation: Moats, caves, and cardboard are real physical things. 'Dragon' is mythical/imaginary. 'Great' expresses a quality that can be exaggerated in imagination. 'Might' expresses a possibility — not yet real.

questions.question: 'Scoop,' 'carve,' and 'zoom' are all verbs — they all describe different ways of moving or doing things.
questions.explanation: Yes! 'Scoop' (pick up with a curved tool), 'carve' (cut carefully), and 'zoom' (move very fast) are all action verbs — they describe different types of actions or movements.

questions.question: 'Skip,' 'zoom,' and 'crawl' all describe movement. Which is SLOWEST to FASTEST?
questions.explanation: Slowest to fastest: Crawl (very slow, on hands and knees) → Skip (moderate, light jumping forward) → Zoom (very fast movement). This is the correct speed order.

questions.question: The poem says 'a great big box' — here 'great' could be replaced with _______ and still mean the same thing.
questions.hint: Another word for very large in size
questions.explanation: In this context, 'great' means 'very large.' 'A great big box' = 'a large/huge/enormous box.' The word 'great' here emphasizes the impressive size of the box.

questions.question: Arrange the words to make a sentence about the theme of imagination:
questions.explanation: Correct sentence: 'Imagination can bring ordinary things to life.' This is the key theme of 'The Box' — a plain cardboard box becomes a cave, castle, and rocket through imagination.

questions.question: The poems use words like 'dragon,' 'moat,' and 'cave' to show that children use everyday things to:
options: Create magical, imaginary worlds from ordinary objects
questions.explanation: A box becomes a 'cave' and 'castle'; sand becomes a 'castle' with a 'moat'; 'dragon' adds magic. These words show how children transform the ordinary into the extraordinary through imagination.
